title: Python Code Written So Far
text:
This page explains the Python code already written for the all_results.py project.
At this stage, the code does not yet parse files or assemble the final result tree. Instead, it defines the first three foundational classes of the architecture:

Interval
FlavorAssignment
ChannelKey

These classes are small, but they establish the vocabulary and structure that the rest of the program will use.


The code begins with:
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from math import isnan
from typing import Optional




This changes how Python handles type hints. It allows the code to use return annotations like "Interval" safely inside the class definition, without requiring the class to be fully defined at the moment the annotation is parsed.
This is useful for methods such as:
@classmethod
def nan(cls) -> "Interval":
    ...

@classmethod
def from_value_error(cls, value: float, error: float) -> "Interval":
    ...



This imports the @dataclass decorator.
A dataclass is a convenient way to define classes whose main purpose is to store structured data. It automatically provides an initializer and other useful methods.
This is appropriate here because these first classes are mostly data containers with a small amount of related behavior.



This imports the isnan function, which is used to check whether a floating-point value is NaN.



This imports Optional, which is used in type hints such as:
f: Optional[str] = None
This means that the attribute may be either a string or None.





The first class is:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Interval:
    min_value: float
    max_value: float

    @classmethod
    def nan(cls) -> "Interval":
        return cls(float("nan"), float("nan"))

    @classmethod
    def from_value_error(cls, value: float, error: float) -> "Interval":
        return cls(value - error, value + error)

    def is_nan(self) -> bool:
        return isnan(self.min_value) and isnan(self.max_value)

    def format(self, decimals: int = 4) -> str:
        if self.is_nan():
            return "[NaN, NaN]"
        return f"[{self.min_value:.{decimals}f}, {self.max_value:.{decimals}f}]"


This class represents a numeric interval of the form:
[min_value, max_value]
In the project, this same structure is needed for:

calculated mass intervals
partial-wave intervals
diquark intervals
missing values, represented as [NaN, NaN]

The design idea is that all interval-like quantities should use the same object instead of being handled separately.



The class is declared as a frozen dataclass:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
This has two consequences:

the class automatically gets an initializer, so Interval(min_value, max_value) works directly
instances are immutable, meaning their fields cannot be changed after creation

Immutability is useful here because an interval is meant to be a fixed mathematical object once created.



The class stores two fields:
min_value: float
max_value: float
These are the lower and upper bounds of the interval.



The first helper is:
@classmethod
def nan(cls) -> "Interval":
    return cls(float("nan"), float("nan"))
This creates the standard missing interval:
[NaN, NaN]
Instead of repeatedly writing:
Interval(float("nan"), float("nan"))
the code can simply call:
Interval.nan()



The second helper is:
@classmethod
def from_value_error(cls, value: float, error: float) -> "Interval":
    return cls(value - error, value + error)
This converts a quantity written as:
value +/- error
into an interval:
[value - error, value + error]
This is exactly the rule needed for the wave and diquark analysis files.
For example, if a contribution is:
0.4237 +/- 0.0062
then the interval becomes:
[0.4175, 0.4299]
This method centralizes that conversion rule in one place.



The method
def is_nan(self) -> bool:
    return isnan(self.min_value) and isnan(self.max_value)
checks whether the interval is the missing-value interval.
This is necessary because later, when formatting output, the code needs to distinguish between an ordinary numeric interval and the special case [NaN, NaN].



The final method is:
def format(self, decimals: int = 4) -> str:
    if self.is_nan():
        return "[NaN, NaN]"
    return f"[{self.min_value:.{decimals}f}, {self.max_value:.{decimals}f}]"
This converts the interval into the string form required for output.
If the interval is missing, it returns:
[NaN, NaN]
Otherwise it formats both endpoints with a chosen number of decimal places. The default is four decimal places, which is especially suitable for wave and diquark contributions.




The second class is:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class FlavorAssignment:
    f: Optional[str] = None
    g: Optional[str] = None
    h: Optional[str] = None

    def code(self) -> Optional[str]:
        parts = [x for x in (self.f, self.g, self.h) if x is not None]
        return "".join(parts) if parts else None

    def is_light_only(self) -> bool:
        return self.code() is None

    def mapping(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        result = {}
        if self.f is not None:
            result["f"] = self.f
        if self.g is not None:
            result["g"] = self.g
        if self.h is not None:
            result["h"] = self.h
        return result

    def substitute_symbols(self, text: str) -> str:
        result = text
        for symbol, flavor in self.mapping().items():
            result = result.replace(symbol, flavor)
        return result


This class represents the heavy-flavor specification attached to a baryon channel.
Examples include:

no heavy flavor at all
one heavy flavor, such as f = s
two heavy flavors, such as f = s, g = c
three heavy flavors, such as f = s, g = c, h = b

The class stores this information in structured form instead of using raw strings like "sc" everywhere.



The class stores up to three heavy-flavor placeholders:
f: Optional[str] = None
g: Optional[str] = None
h: Optional[str] = None
Each of these may contain a physical flavor such as:

"s"
"c"
"b"

or may be None if that placeholder is not used.
Examples:

light-only baryon: f=None, g=None, h=None
one-heavy-flavor case: f="s", g=None, h=None
two-heavy-flavor case: f="s", g="c", h=None
three-heavy-flavor case: f="s", g="c", h="b"




The method
def code(self) -> Optional[str]:
    parts = [x for x in (self.f, self.g, self.h) if x is not None]
    return "".join(parts) if parts else None
builds the compact code corresponding to the assignment.
Examples:

None, None, None -> None
f="s" -> "s"
f="s", g="c" -> "sc"
f="s", g="c", h="b" -> "scb"

This compact form is useful because it matches the channel naming used in:

filenames
APMEB_Summary.txt
human-readable labels later on




The method
def is_light_only(self) -> bool:
    return self.code() is None
checks whether the baryon has no heavy-flavor assignment.
This is useful because the output structure differs between:

light-only baryons such as Delta and Nucleon
baryons with heavy-flavor subsections




The method
def mapping(self) -> dict[str, str]:
    result = {}
    if self.f is not None:
        result["f"] = self.f
    if self.g is not None:
        result["g"] = self.g
    if self.h is not None:
        result["h"] = self.h
    return result
builds a dictionary mapping symbolic placeholders to physical flavors.
Examples:

f="s" gives {"f": "s"}
f="s", g="c" gives {"f": "s", "g": "c"}
f="s", g="c", h="b" gives {"f": "s", "g": "c", "h": "b"}

This is a useful intermediate representation for later substitutions.



The final method is:
def substitute_symbols(self, text: str) -> str:
    result = text
    for symbol, flavor in self.mapping().items():
        result = result.replace(symbol, flavor)
    return result
This replaces symbolic flavor labels with physical ones.
For example:

SC(nf) becomes SC(ns) when f = s
AV(fg) becomes AV(sc) when f = s and g = c

This method will later be important when writing the final output file, because the diquark labels should appear in their physical form.




The third class is:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ChannelKey:
    flavor_assignment: FlavorAssignment
    J: str
    parity: str

    def j_code(self) -> str:
        if self.J == "1/2":
            return "J12"
        if self.J == "3/2":
            return "J32"
        raise ValueError(f"Unsupported J value: {self.J}")

    def prefix(self) -> str:
        flavor_code = self.flavor_assignment.code()

        if flavor_code is None:
            return f"{self.j_code()}_{self.parity}_"

        return f"[{flavor_code}]_{self.j_code()}_{self.parity}_"

    def summary_channel_name(self) -> str:
        return self.prefix().rstrip("_")

    def display_label(self) -> str:
        return f"J = {self.J}, Parity = {self.parity}"


This class represents the identity of a baryon channel.
A channel is determined by:

heavy-flavor assignment
total angular momentum J
parity

Examples of channels include:

J = 1/2, positive parity, light-only
[s]_J32_Negative
[sc]_J12_Positive

This object centralizes the logic for converting that identity into the various string forms used in the project.



The class stores:
flavor_assignment: FlavorAssignment
J: str
parity: str
So a ChannelKey is a structured combination of:

one FlavorAssignment
one J value
one parity value




The method
def j_code(self) -> str:
    if self.J == "1/2":
        return "J12"
    if self.J == "3/2":
        return "J32"
    raise ValueError(f"Unsupported J value: {self.J}")
converts the human-readable J value into the file-naming code.
Specifically:

"1/2" -> "J12"
"3/2" -> "J32"

If another value is given, the method raises an error. That is useful because it enforces the current project assumptions explicitly.



The method
def prefix(self) -> str:
    flavor_code = self.flavor_assignment.code()

    if flavor_code is None:
        return f"{self.j_code()}_{self.parity}_"

    return f"[{flavor_code}]_{self.j_code()}_{self.parity}_"
builds the filename prefix used by the wave and diquark files.
Examples:

light-only: J12_Positive_
one heavy flavor: [s]_J32_Negative_
two heavy flavors: [sc]_J12_Positive_

This is one of the most important naming utilities in the code, because it gives the exact prefix needed to locate input files.



The method
def summary_channel_name(self) -> str:
    return self.prefix().rstrip("_")
builds the channel name as it appears in APMEB_Summary.txt.
The summary file uses the same naming convention as the analysis files, except without the trailing underscore.
Examples:

J12_Positive
[s]_J32_Negative
[sc]_J12_Positive

This method avoids duplicating that small but important naming rule elsewhere.



The method
def display_label(self) -> str:
    return f"J = {self.J}, Parity = {self.parity}"
builds the human-readable label intended for All_Results.txt.
Example:
J = 1/2, Parity = Positive
This is different from the filename form and the summary-file form, so it is useful to keep all three naming styles separated.




These classes were written first because they provide the minimal shared vocabulary needed by the rest of the program.

Interval defines how numeric intervals are represented
FlavorAssignment defines how heavy-flavor structure is represented
ChannelKey defines how a channel is identified and named

All later parts of the program will build on these concepts.
For example:

parsers will create Interval objects
metadata classes will create valid FlavorAssignment objects
channels throughout the program will be indexed by ChannelKey

These classes were written first because they provide the minimal shared vocabulary needed by the rest of the program.

Interval defines how numeric intervals are represented

title: Home
text:
This website documents the code for parsing baryon results, located in multipled .txt files, into one .txt file called All_Results.txt.



Input data
Output structure
OOP architecture
Implementation log

title: Input Data Sources
text:
This page documents the input files and naming conventions used by the parsing project. The goal is to explain where the raw data lives, how the files are named, and how the parser matches the different inputs for each baryon channel.


The parent directory contains:

the Python parsing code
APMEB_Summary.txt
the baryons/ folder




Inside baryons/, there are 12 baryon-category folders:

Delta
Lambda_f
Nucleon
Omega_fff
Omega_ffg
Omega_fgg
Omega_fgh
OmegaPrime_fgh
Sigma_f
Xi_ff
Xi_fg
XiPrime_fg

This organization uses the symbols f, g, and h to represent heavy flavors. In practice, the baryons fall into the following groups:

Three equal quark flavors: Nucleon, Delta, Omega_fff
Two equal quark flavors plus one heavier flavor: Lambda_f, Sigma_f, Omega_ffg
Two equal quark flavors plus one lighter flavor: Xi_ff, Omega_fgg
Three different quark flavors: Xi_fg, XiPrime_fg, Omega_fgh, OmegaPrime_fgh






Baryons without an underscore contain only the light flavor n. Here n represents the light up/down sector, since the calculation does not distinguish those flavors through purely gluonic interactions.
The baryons in this class are:

Delta
Nucleon

Different baryons in this class arise from different isospin values.



Baryons with only f after the underscore contain one heavy-flavor type. The physical heavy flavor can be:

strange (s)
charm (c)
bottom (b)

The baryons that follow this convention are:

Lambda_f
Sigma_f
Xi_ff
Omega_fff




Baryons with f and g contain two distinct heavy flavors. These baryons are:

Xi_fg
XiPrime_fg
Omega_ffg
Omega_fgg




Baryons with f, g, and h contain three distinct heavy flavors. These baryons are:

Omega_fgh
OmegaPrime_fgh





The symbolic ordering is always:
f < g < h
This means the heavy flavors are always listed from lighter to heavier. For example, sc is allowed, but cs is not.
The allowed physical assignments are:

One heavy flavor: s, c, b
Two heavy flavors: sc, sb, cb
Three heavy flavors: scb




Each channel name is built from:

a heavy-flavor specification, when applicable
a J value
a parity value

These parts are combined into the filename prefix used inside the analysis folders.


Light-only baryons have no heavy-flavor bracket.
For heavy-flavor baryons, the bracketed prefixes are:

One heavy flavor: [s]_, [c]_, [b]_
Two heavy flavors: [sc]_, [sb]_, [cb]_
Three heavy flavors: [scb]_




The allowed J values are:

1/2, written in filenames as J12_
3/2, written in filenames as J32_




The allowed parity values are:

Positive_
Negative_





Examples of complete channel prefixes are:

J12_Positive_
J32_Negative_
[s]_J12_Positive_
[c]_J12_Negative_
[sc]_J32_Positive_
[scb]_J32_Negative_




For a given baryon and valid channel prefix, the relevant files are:


baryons/<BARYON>/analysis_waves/<PREFIX>PartWavs_Analysis.txt
These files contain the partial-wave contributions for the four states.



baryons/<BARYON>/analysis_diquarks/<PREFIX>DQ_Analysis.txt
These files contain the diquark contributions for the four states.




APMEB_Summary.txt is located in the root directory and provides the calculated mass intervals.



The first 2 lines are header metadata and can be ignored.
The rest of the file is organized into repeated baryon blocks.
Each baryon block begins after an empty line and a separator line.
The baryon block header has the form:

=== Baryon: <BARYON> ===



Inside each baryon block, there are repeated channel sub-blocks. Each channel sub-block corresponds to a channel prefix without the trailing underscore.
Examples:

J12_Positive
[sc]_J32_Negative

Each channel sub-block contains:
Channel: <CHANNEL>
Grd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
1st : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
2nd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
3rd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
There is an empty line between channel sub-blocks.




These files contain partial-wave contributions.



The first 4 lines can be ignored.
Then come 4 state blocks:
Grd
1st
2nd
3rd

Each state block begins with:
State: <STATE> (i=<INDEX>)
Each state block ends with a dashed separator line.



Each state block contains the fixed partial-wave components:

s-wave
p-wave
d-wave
f-wave

Each contribution line has the form:
<component> : <value> +/- <error> (<method>)
Then there is a final line:
SUM : <value>



For the final project representation:

minimum wave value = value - error
maximum wave value = value + error





These files contain diquark contributions.


The structure is the same as in PartWavs_Analysis.txt:

the first 4 lines are ignored
there are 4 state blocks
each block begins with State: <STATE> (i=<INDEX>)
each block ends with a dashed separator line
each contribution line has the form <component> : <value> +/- <error> (<method>)
each block ends with SUM : <value>




For the final project representation:

minimum diquark value = value - error
maximum diquark value = value + error







SC(nn)
AV(nn)





SC(nn)
SC(nf)
AV(nn)
AV(nf)





SC(nf)
SC(ff)
AV(nf)
AV(ff)





SC(ff)
AV(ff)





SC(nf)
SC(ng)
SC(fg)
AV(nf)
AV(ng)
AV(fg)





SC(ff)
SC(fg)
AV(ff)
AV(fg)





SC(fg)
SC(gg)
AV(fg)
AV(gg)





SC(fg)
SC(fh)
SC(gh)
AV(fg)
AV(fh)
AV(gh)






For each expected combination of baryon, heavy-flavor assignment, J, and parity:

APMEB_Summary.txt provides the mass intervals for Grd, 1st, 2nd, and 3rd
the matching PartWavs_Analysis.txt file provides the partial-wave data for the same four states
the matching DQ_Analysis.txt file provides the diquark data for the same four states

If any expected channel or quantity is missing, the final output will use:
[NaN, NaN]

title: Input Data Sources
text:
Baryons without an underscore contain only the light flavor n. Here n represents the light up/down sector, since the calculation does not distinguish those flavors through purely gluonic interactions.
The baryons in this class are:

Delta
Nucleon

Different baryons in this class arise from different isospin values.



Baryons with only f after the underscore contain one heavy-flavor type. The physical heavy flavor can be:

strange (s)
charm (c)
bottom (b)

The baryons that follow this convention are:

Lambda_f
Sigma_f
Xi_ff
Omega_fff




Baryons with f and g contain two distinct heavy flavors. These baryons are:

Xi_fg
XiPrime_fg
Omega_ffg
Omega_fgg




Baryons with f, g, and h contain three distinct heavy flavors. These baryons are:

Omega_fgh
OmegaPrime_fgh

title: Input Data Sources
text:
The symbolic ordering is always:
f < g < h
This means the heavy flavors are always listed from lighter to heavier. For example, sc is allowed, but cs is not.
The allowed physical assignments are:

One heavy flavor: s, c, b
Two heavy flavors: sc, sb, cb
Three heavy flavors: scb

title: Input Data Sources
text:
Each channel name is built from:

a heavy-flavor specification, when applicable
a J value
a parity value

These parts are combined into the filename prefix used inside the analysis folders.


Light-only baryons have no heavy-flavor bracket.
For heavy-flavor baryons, the bracketed prefixes are:

One heavy flavor: [s]_, [c]_, [b]_
Two heavy flavors: [sc]_, [sb]_, [cb]_
Three heavy flavors: [scb]_




The allowed J values are:

1/2, written in filenames as J12_
3/2, written in filenames as J32_




The allowed parity values are:

Positive_
Negative_

title: Input Data Sources
text:
Examples of complete channel prefixes are:

J12_Positive_
J32_Negative_
[s]_J12_Positive_
[c]_J12_Negative_
[sc]_J32_Positive_
[scb]_J32_Negative_

title: Input Data Sources
text:
For a given baryon and valid channel prefix, the relevant files are:


baryons/<BARYON>/analysis_waves/<PREFIX>PartWavs_Analysis.txt
These files contain the partial-wave contributions for the four states.



baryons/<BARYON>/analysis_diquarks/<PREFIX>DQ_Analysis.txt
These files contain the diquark contributions for the four states.

title: Input Data Sources
text:
APMEB_Summary.txt is located in the root directory and provides the calculated mass intervals.



The first 2 lines are header metadata and can be ignored.
The rest of the file is organized into repeated baryon blocks.
Each baryon block begins after an empty line and a separator line.
The baryon block header has the form:

=== Baryon: <BARYON> ===



Inside each baryon block, there are repeated channel sub-blocks. Each channel sub-block corresponds to a channel prefix without the trailing underscore.
Examples:

J12_Positive
[sc]_J32_Negative

Each channel sub-block contains:
Channel: <CHANNEL>
Grd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
1st : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
2nd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
3rd : [min, max] MeV (Width: width MeV)
There is an empty line between channel sub-blocks.

title: Input Data Sources
text:
These files contain partial-wave contributions.



The first 4 lines can be ignored.
Then come 4 state blocks:
Grd
1st
2nd
3rd

Each state block begins with:
State: <STATE> (i=<INDEX>)
Each state block ends with a dashed separator line.



Each state block contains the fixed partial-wave components:

s-wave
p-wave
d-wave
f-wave

Each contribution line has the form:
<component> : <value> +/- <error> (<method>)
Then there is a final line:
SUM : <value>



For the final project representation:

minimum wave value = value - error
maximum wave value = value + error

title: Input Data Sources
text:
These files contain diquark contributions.


The structure is the same as in PartWavs_Analysis.txt:

the first 4 lines are ignored
there are 4 state blocks
each block begins with State: <STATE> (i=<INDEX>)
each block ends with a dashed separator line
each contribution line has the form <component> : <value> +/- <error> (<method>)
each block ends with SUM : <value>




For the final project representation:

minimum diquark value = value - error
maximum diquark value = value + error







SC(nn)
AV(nn)





SC(nn)
SC(nf)
AV(nn)
AV(nf)





SC(nf)
SC(ff)
AV(nf)
AV(ff)





SC(ff)
AV(ff)





SC(nf)
SC(ng)
SC(fg)
AV(nf)
AV(ng)
AV(fg)





SC(ff)
SC(fg)
AV(ff)
AV(fg)





SC(fg)
SC(gg)
AV(fg)
AV(gg)





SC(fg)
SC(fh)
SC(gh)
AV(fg)
AV(fh)
AV(gh)

title: Input Data Sources
text:
For each expected combination of baryon, heavy-flavor assignment, J, and parity:

APMEB_Summary.txt provides the mass intervals for Grd, 1st, 2nd, and 3rd
the matching PartWavs_Analysis.txt file provides the partial-wave data for the same four states
the matching DQ_Analysis.txt file provides the diquark data for the same four states

If any expected channel or quantity is missing, the final output will use:
[NaN, NaN]

title: Architecture Plan
text:
This page records the current software architecture plan for the all_results.py project.


The project aims to build a Python program that reads:

APMEB_Summary.txt
PartWavs_Analysis.txt
DQ_Analysis.txt

from the baryon folder hierarchy and produces a normalized All_Results.txt file containing:

calculated mass intervals
partial-wave intervals
diquark intervals

for all expected baryons, heavy-flavor assignments, J values, parities, and states, using [NaN, NaN] where data is missing.



The code is organized around the normalized scientific object we want to produce, not around the raw input files. Parsing, data modeling, orchestration, and text output are separated into distinct layers.

title: Architecture Plan
text:
The project aims to build a Python program that reads:

APMEB_Summary.txt
PartWavs_Analysis.txt
DQ_Analysis.txt

from the baryon folder hierarchy and produces a normalized All_Results.txt file containing:

calculated mass intervals
partial-wave intervals
diquark intervals

for all expected baryons, heavy-flavor assignments, J values, parities, and states, using [NaN, NaN] where data is missing.

title: Architecture Plan
text: The code is organized around the normalized scientific object we want to produce, not around the raw input files. Parsing, data modeling, orchestration, and text output are separated into distinct layers.

title: Architecture Plan
text:
1. Domain model
These classes represent the normalized in-memory scientific data independently of how it was read from disk.

Interval
Purpose:

represent any numeric interval used in the project
masses, partial waves, diquarks, and missing values all use the same object

What it solves:

gives the project a single reusable interval representation
avoids separate handling for masses, waves, and diquarks



FlavorAssignment
Purpose:

represent the heavy-flavor assignment associated with a baryon channel in a structured way

What it solves:

centralizes heavy-flavor logic
avoids manipulating raw flavor strings throughout the code



ChannelKey
Purpose:

represent the identity of a baryon channel through heavy-flavor assignment, J, and parity

What it solves:

gives the program one immutable channel identifier
centralizes naming conventions for files, summary keys, and output labels



StateResult
Purpose:

represent all output information associated with one state (Grd, 1st, 2nd, 3rd) of one specific channel

What it will solve:

collect all state-level data into one object so the writer can emit the final text format directly from normalized data



ChannelResult
Purpose:

represent the four state results belonging to one specific channel

What it will solve:

group the four state results under a single channel identity



BaryonResult
Purpose:

represent all channels belonging to one baryon category

What it will solve:

provide a baryon-level container matching the final baryon-block structure in All_Results.txt



AllResults
Purpose:

represent the complete normalized result set across the whole project

What it will solve:

act as the top-level object passed to the writer to generate All_Results.txt

title: Architecture Plan
text:
2. Project metadata
These classes encode the scientific and structural rules of the project independently of parsed data.

BaryonSpec
Purpose:

describe one baryon category, including heavy-flavor pattern, composition pattern, and diquark types

What it will solve:

prevents baryon-specific rules from being scattered through parsers, assembler, and writer



ProjectCatalog
Purpose:

hold the complete registry of baryon specifications and the global ordering rules

What it will solve:

provides a single authoritative source for what should exist, including channels currently missing in the data

title: Architecture Plan
text:
3. Parsers
These read raw text files and convert them into normalized intermediate data.

APMEBSummaryParser
Purpose:

parse APMEB_Summary.txt
extract calculated mass intervals keyed by baryon, channel, and state

What it will solve:

isolate all logic related to the summary-file format



WaveAnalysisParser
Purpose:

parse one PartWavs_Analysis.txt file
extract per-state wave intervals derived from value +/- error

What it will solve:

convert wave contribution lines into Interval objects with uniform representation



DiquarkAnalysisParser
Purpose:

parse one DQ_Analysis.txt file
extract per-state diquark intervals derived from value +/- error

What it will solve:

convert baryon-dependent diquark contribution lines into Interval objects with uniform representation

title: Architecture Plan
text:
4. Assembly

ResultsAssembler
Purpose:

iterate over all expected baryons, flavor assignments, channels, and states
load parsed information
populate the normalized result tree

Responsibilities:

read mass intervals from APMEB summary data
read wave intervals from matching analysis files
read diquark intervals from matching analysis files
fill missing quantities with [NaN, NaN]
attach WIP placeholders for PDG and LatticeQCD intervals

What it will solve:

centralize orchestration logic so parsers remain simple and the writer only sees normalized data

title: Architecture Plan
text:
5. Output

AllResultsWriter
Purpose:

write the final hierarchical text file with the required indentation, labels, ordering, and separators

Responsibilities:

write baryon blocks
write heavy-flavor subsections where applicable
write channel sections in fixed order
write state sections in fixed order
render intervals and placeholders
render physical diquark labels after flavor substitution

What it will solve:

keeps formatting concerns separate from parsing and data representation

title: Output File Structure
text:
This page describes the structure of the final output file produced by the project:
All_Results.txt
The goal of this file is to provide a complete normalized summary of all baryon results, including:

calculated mass intervals
placeholders for PDG and LatticeQCD intervals
partial-wave contribution intervals
diquark contribution intervals

If a quantity is missing from the input data, the interval
[NaN, NaN]
is written.
PDG and LatticeQCD intervals are currently placeholders and are written as:
WIP


The file is organized hierarchically:
Baryon
  Heavy flavor assignment (if applicable)
    Channel (J, parity)
      State
        Mass intervals
        Wave intervals
        Diquark intervals



The top-level unit of the file is the baryon block.
Each baryon block begins with:
Baryon: <BARYON>
Examples:

Baryon: Nucleon
Baryon: Delta
Baryon: Sigma_f

Each baryon block ends with a separator:
=============================================================================



For baryons with only light flavors (n), the relevant cases are:

Delta
Nucleon

These baryon blocks directly contain the channel sections.
Example structure:
Baryon: Nucleon
  J = 1/2, Parity = Positive
    State = Grd (Ground)
    Calculated mass interval = [MIN, MAX]
    PDG mass interval = WIP
    LatticeQCD mass interval = WIP
    Partial waves distributions:
      s-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
      p-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
      d-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
      f-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
    Diquark distributions:
      SC(nn) interval = [MIN, MAX]
      AV(nn) interval = [MIN, MAX]
    -----------------------------------------------------------------------------



Baryons containing heavy flavors include an additional subsection:
Heavy flavor specification: ...
Examples:

Heavy flavor specification: f = s (Forming nns)
Heavy flavor specification: f = c (Forming nnc)
Heavy flavor specification: f = s, g = c (Forming nsc)
Heavy flavor specification: f = s, g = c, h = b (Forming scb)

This subsection groups the channel results for that specific flavor assignment.
Example structure:
Baryon: Sigma_f
  Heavy flavor specification: f = s (Forming nns)
    J = 1/2, Parity = Positive
      ...



Inside each heavy-flavor subsection, or directly inside the baryon block for light baryons, the file contains four channel sections.
The allowed channels are:

J = 1/2, Parity = Positive
J = 1/2, Parity = Negative
J = 3/2, Parity = Positive
J = 3/2, Parity = Negative

Example:
J = 1/2, Parity = Positive
Each channel contains four state sections.



Each channel contains the four states:

Grd
1st
2nd
3rd

These labels correspond to:



Label
Meaning




Grd
Ground state


1st
First excited state


2nd
Second excited state


3rd
Third excited state



Example state header:
State = Grd (Ground)



Each state begins with the mass information:
Calculated mass interval = [MIN, MAX]
PDG mass interval = WIP
LatticeQCD mass interval = WIP
Example:
Calculated mass interval = [938.0, 940.0]
PDG mass interval = WIP
LatticeQCD mass interval = WIP



The next section lists the partial-wave contributions:
Partial waves distributions:
There are always four components:

s-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
p-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
d-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]
f-wave interval = [MIN, MAX]

These intervals are derived from input values of the form
value +/- error
using:

min = value - error
max = value + error




The diquark section follows:
Diquark distributions:
The number and type of diquark components depend on the baryon.
Example:

SC(nn) interval = [MIN, MAX]
AV(nn) interval = [MIN, MAX]

For heavy-flavor baryons, the symbolic labels are replaced using the physical flavors.
Example substitutions:

SC(nf) -> SC(ns) when f = s
AV(fg) -> AV(sc) when f = s and g = c




Each state block ends with:
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
This separator appears after the diquark section.



The file follows fixed ordering rules.



Delta
Lambda_f
Nucleon
Omega_fff
Omega_ffg
Omega_fgg
Omega_fgh
OmegaPrime_fgh
Sigma_f
Xi_ff
Xi_fg
XiPrime_fg





J = 1/2, Positive
J = 1/2, Negative
J = 3/2, Positive
J = 3/2, Negative





Grd
1st
2nd
3rd





s-wave
p-wave
d-wave
f-wave





If a quantity cannot be found in the input files, the program writes:
[NaN, NaN]
Example:
s-wave interval = [NaN, NaN]
This ensures the output file maintains a complete and consistent structure even when some channels have not yet been computed.

text:

title: About
text:
Purpose
This site is built with Quarto and deployed with GitHub pages. This was a learning oportunity for:

Programming with OOP in Python for a quite simple task that helps tremendously in writing my PhD research documentation
Using Quarto to write documentation via markdown-like syntax
Using GitHub pages that, with Quarto, deploys these files as html
